item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I shall fix that wording! Not Falkirk's, though - I know the sound is hearing-damaging and picked that number intentionally. Falkirk dismissing it as an "inconvenience" is him being a dick because he thinks he's the only one being professional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this! One quick nitpick, though.

SCP-5056-A emits a constant tone of 119 decibels

Decibels is a loudness thing, not a pitch thing, so it should either say "at 119 decibels," or "of 119 hertz" if you do mean pitch.
Also, if you do in fact mean decibels, you should know that 119 decibels is extremely loud and potentially damaging to the ears, not just an "inconvenience" like Falkirk says in the footnotes of the test log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doug's happy to let you know how he feels, he just requires one of your eyes in payment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, I'll definitely read it! He's interesting and seems really harmless, out at least as harmless as most humans, UNTIL something happens that might cause Phil harm.

BUT if Dr Falkirk hadn't gone too far then they may have never known more about his abilities. 😁

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for reading, and I'm glad you liked it!

One of my earliest thoughts for Doug's motivations was that he was an alien intelligence attempting to show concern in an alien manner, which translates to unending torment for poor, beloved Phil. I've made some tweaks along the way, but that core idea has stuck.

I'm glad you noticed the brother thing; I've intentionally left that a bit vague. There's a few things like that in this article, subtle hints for why things happen the way they do.

There's a very long tale in two parts about Phil and Doug. I think it's worth the read, if you want to see more of that relationship. But then, I'm biased aren't I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on the fence with this until The Incident (Incident 5056-I-04), now I am fully on Doug's side! Even though Doug seems to harangue -B it seems like he is trying to be somewhat protective of his corporeal counterpart, and I'll admit to a certain amount of schadenfreude with what happens to to Dr Falkirk.

It's interesting that we don't see any research into his relationship with his brother, especially seeing the similarities between the name he has given -A (Doug) and his brother's name (Dougall).

+1

Moral of the story, don't mess with Philip! 😁

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Pexels is not considered an acceptable source anymore, due to the Pexels license not being compatible with CC BY-SA 3.0. However, the glasses image is still considered compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0, as it was uploaded to Pixabay in 2016, before the Pixabay license was introduced, and is thus public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, thanks. And no pressure, but that would be awesome!

This one's got a special place in my heart, and I've got a tale coming eventually to flesh out my saddest married couple ever :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really fun one, I might draw this sometime!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually been a month and ten days - those "J" months trip me up too! But it has changed a lot. It was my first skip, and I've been going back and streamlining it as I get the hang of the format because it means quite a bit to me that it's survived. I'm glad you think it works, and thanks for the kind words! The first unambiguously positive sentiments it's received since I posted the initial draft to the forum :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
huh, this seems to have changed quite a bit in the grand total of ten days it's been on the site, going by the conversations in here.

Anyway, I liked it. It's hard to do a decently interesting spookyman SCP these days but you've pulled it off

+1

item.title: Re: Licensing JS Post.
item.content:encoded: I've reformatted my image credits to include hyperlinks to the original files.

item.title: Licensing JS Post.
item.content:encoded: Author, you are required to properly cite the sources of the images used within your article. Simply saying that the images used are royalty free is not an acceptable way to cite your sources.

item.title: Re: Issue with classification
item.content:encoded: No problem!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comments!

I'll consider replacing some of the instances of Deering's name; some of them make sense chronologically, but there's probably room for improvement.

The employee numbers are actually in service of a bit of a dig at the end - they show that by 2019 the janitors have four-digit employee numbers, while this poor schmoe is still around being JM64. But perhaps the audience is reminded of those numbers too often for the payoff to justify. I'll take it under consideration!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a decent article.

The formatting is a bit odd for sure, but I think the content make sup for that.

Though the seemingly excessive usage of names throughout the article, instead of designations, like XXXX-B in their place, in addition to the large use of repeated employee ID numbers, which should probably be mentioned once in the foot notes, you know?

item.title: Re: Issue with classification
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for that; I think the classification has now been discussed enough for me to change it to straight Euclid and alter the language in the article to remove the Thaumiel references. I thought it was a neat idea, but it's probably a neat idea for a different topic.

Glad you liked the idea though!

item.title: Issue with classification
item.content:encoded: I do enjoy the trope of a single human containing an anomaly, but making them into a Thaumiel for it is kinda unnecessary to me. Thaumiel-class anomalies are usually much larger scale ones, such as preventing worldwide disasters or providing immense aid to the Foundation. A single human - especially a non-anomalous one - containing a single anomaly doesn't really fit into that criteria for me. Could be just a personal opinion, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The figure standing in the background was the only reason for the image to exist. Since I already thought it looked cheesy, and apparently it precipitated a don't-read-and-downvote, it had to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry to be blunt, but the image could honestly do without the figure standing in the background. Everything else looks fine in the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …ow. Well, it's gone now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That image looks like a deep-fried meme but without the ironic impact font.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
582 revisions later, here we are.

Proof of greenlights and proof of critiques/drafting.

All images are altered royalty-free stock except the first, which is just me.

Many thanks to TheSandwichMan, Sonderance, Boogey_Man23 and r0se for their help!

One image has been replaced because of license change, indicated in blue.

"Phil" is borrowed from:

Original Source: flickr
Creator: dcpetit
License: CC BY-SA 2.02
